title: Target Vocabulary Management
concept: Managing output vocabularies (e.g. projecting hidden states to 50,000 word logits) is the primary memory bottleneck in decoders.

exercises.prompt: What is a primary memory bottleneck when a decoder model generates a word?

options.text: Managing a very large output vocabulary
Reading the input text in reverse

exercises.explain: Decoders must map their internal hidden states to a very large list of potential words (the vocabulary) to generate text, which requires significant memory.

cards.title: The Final Word Bottleneck
cards.body: Have you ever wondered why AI models sometimes run out of memory right at the moment they try to speak? It's not the thinking part that breaks them—it's the massive dictionary they have to choose their words from!

cards.title: Target Vocabulary Management
cards.body: To output text, a decoder must translate its internal mathematical thoughts (hidden states) into actual words. It does this by projecting these states into a massive list of scores (logits) for every single word in its vocabulary—often 50,000 words or more. Storing and computing this massive final step is the primary memory bottleneck in decoders.

cards.title: The Stamp Wall Dilemma
cards.body: Imagine a cashier at a massive hypermarket. Instead of typing in a simple price code, they have to physically reach for one of 50,000 different branded stamps on a giant wall for every single item they scan. The cashier spends almost all their energy and time looking up and grabbing those stamps, rather than scanning the items themselves!

cards.title: Inside the Prediction Step
cards.body: When a model like GPT predicts the next word, it doesn't just pick one instantly. It creates a list of 50,000+ numbers representing the probability of every possible word in its dictionary. This final calculation requires a massive weight matrix that eats up valuable GPU memory.

cards.title: The Vocabulary Myth
cards.body: Many people think that understanding long sentences is the only thing that slows down an AI. In reality, just having a huge vocabulary (like 50,000+ words) creates a massive memory roadblock at the very last step of generating each word.

cards.title: Vector Size vs. Vocabulary Size
cards.body: Compare how the memory footprint changes between the internal thinking steps and the final word selection step.

left.text: Compact vector representations (e.g., size 4,096) used during reasoning.

right.text: Massive expansion to match the vocabulary size (e.g., 50,000+ logits).

exercises.prompt: Why does the final vocabulary projection stage create a major memory bottleneck in decoders?

options.text: It requires mapping internal states to tens of thousands of word logits.
It uses too many layers of self-attention.
It only happens at the beginning of the prompt.
It ignores the vocabulary list completely.

exercises.explain: Decoders must project their internal representations to a massive vocabulary list (often 50,000+ words), which requires a huge amount of memory.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Projecting hidden states to a massive target vocabulary is a major memory bottleneck in decoder models.
exercises.explain: Managing a massive target vocabulary (like 50,000+ words) is indeed the primary memory bottleneck during the final step of text generation.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of the memory bottleneck in decoders.
exercises.sentence: The final projection step is a bottleneck because it must map hidden states to the entire target size.
exercises.explain: Projecting internal hidden states to a huge vocabulary size requires a massive amount of memory, creating a bottleneck.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI component that is responsible for the vocabulary memory bottleneck:
exercises.explain: The output vocabulary projection is the final step that maps hidden states to word choices, whereas attention and feed-forward layers happen earlier in the network.

exercises.prompt: Which operation typically acts as the primary memory bottleneck at the final step of a decoder LLM?

options.text: Projecting hidden states to the massive target vocabulary size
Loading the initial input word embeddings
Calculating the activation functions in feed-forward networks

exercises.explain: Output vocabulary management (projecting hidden vectors to match the massive vocabulary size) dominates memory consumption because it requires maintaining and calculating over a massive matrix (often 50,000+ words) at the final step.
